Timothy Rayner  

These two volumes, The Legacy and Promise of Hans Loewald and The Emerging Tradition of Hans Loewald, offer a remedy for the gap between the esteem in which Loewald is held by psychoanalysts and the general familiarity with his body of work. In my own analytic training, which I suspect was not unique, the only paper by Loewald assigned was his 1960 paper, “On the Therapeutic Action of Psychoanalysis.” Loewald's Psychoanalysis and the History of the Individual was published in 1978. He later added two substantial monographs, Papers on Psychoanalysis (Loewald, 1980) and Sublimation (Loewald, 1988b). These were compiled into one volume as The Essential Loewald in 2000, with a extensive introduction by Jonathon Lear. Gerald Fogel published The Work of Hans Loewald: An Introduction and Commentary in 1991. To these, I would add Nancy Chodorow's The Psychoanalytic Ear and the Sociological Eye, in 2020. While this last book is not entirely about Loewald, Chodorow identifies an Independent American Tradition of Intersubjective Ego Psychology, with Loewald and Erik Erikson as its primary progenitors.

Franco De Masi  

Analysts usually shy away from the idea of taking a psychotic patient into therapy because, unlike some other disorders, psychotic illness takes a progressive course and can devastate the entire personality. It is as if psychosis represents, in psychoanalysis, an unknown and unknowable territory. This is why we welcome with interest the appearance of From Breakdown to Breakthrough, written by Danielle Knafo and Michael Selzer, which deals with the theme of psychosis and its psychoanalytic treatment. The intent of my commentary is to explore what hypothesis the two authors advance as to the nature of psychotic disorders and if there are new elements that can help us better decipher the enigma of this mysterious illness.

In my view, one of the reasons why psychoanalysis encounters difficulties with patients with psychosis is that it is difficult to understand psychotic functioning because we usually work with neurotic patients who function, as Freud taught us, using the dynamic unconscious, with processes such as repression, symbolization, conflict, and oedipal issues. These dynamics, in my view, are not present in patients with psychosis. In psychosis, the dynamic unconscious is paralyzed and does not allow intuitive and symbolic functioning. A voice or an illumination unveils a new reality to the patient, who believes that this new perception undeniably bears the truth. In psychosis, the mind proceeds by sensorial functioning, by sensorial revelations and not by intuitions, disrupting the patient's ability to think and dream.

As analytic therapists, we are daily exposed to myriad experiences—the patient's and ours. We hear a tantalizing piece of information, a fragment of narrative; at another moment, we are flooded with affect, and at the best times, there is a coming together of affect, understanding, and relatedness. When I'm asked, “How do you do this work year after year?” I often answer, “I get to sit all day and listen to stories and be part of them.” The innocent psychoanalytic office is the scene of endlessly varied dramas. And from the earliest writing of Freud, this same sensibility can be applied to experience outside the office. We are among the few generalists that remain. The breadth of psychoanalysis is potentially one of its great contributions to community life. Because we are constrained by confidentiality, this richness is rarely shown. Alternatively, it can be demonstrated through sensitive application to other realms.

Applied analytic thinkers have a wide range. In an era of superspecialization, analysts can meaningfully pursue subjects from neurobiology to politics to the humanities. David Fisher exemplifies this breadth. In this volume of reviews, memoirs, interviews, and original papers, he organizes disparate subjects under the idea of psychoanalysis as subversive. From the title, I expected Fisher to take on shibboleths of our field, or to use psychoanalysis as a radical perspective on issues and assumptions in other realms. Not the case; Fisher's “subversive” is more nuanced, mining a rich lode of subject matter with the special perspective of a historian-analyst. Idealization of Freud has dominated much of the history of psychoanalysis, although the intensity has certainly dimmed over time. Devaluation of Freud has often characterized external reactions to his work, especially in the field of psychology. So, where do things stand now? Between the extremes of adulation and condemnation, a new option emerges that welcomes multiple perspectives, and, more pointedly, embraces Freud ambivalently.

On the Couch, edited by Andrew Blauner, aspires to offer fresh assessments of Freud, and many of the essays contained in the volume attempt to cultivate an ambivalent stance. Blauner's stated intention for the book is to invite a wide range of writers “to analyze the great analyst” (p. ix). Blauner is a literary agent and editor of several books on diverse topics; he does not reveal much about his interest in psychoanalysis. He does inform us, though, that he “wanted to help get and share a better understanding of the person who has helped us understand ourselves perhaps more than anyone in history” (p. xi).

Rather than trying to cover each of the essays, I want to explore some of the main themes across the 25 essays contained in the volume, and then to return to the idea of working through ambivalence. Several essays take up Freud's character (like Rivka Galchen on his relationship to dogs) and legacy (Sarah Boxer, Susie Orbach, Peter Kramer, Sherry Turkle, Adam Gopnik, and Siri Hustvedt) as well as his family relations (Casey Schwartz on Anna Freud; Daphne Merkin on Martha Freud; and Suzy Boyt and

Authenticity and Reciprocity: Dialogues With Ferenczi is an in-depth discussion by 12 Italian analysts and 1 Spanish analyst of Ferenczi's (1988) Clinical Diary, or as the subtitle of the book indicates, dialogues with his diary. While the book consists of multiple dialogues with the theoretical and clinical material published in the diary, it reads more like a four-way dialogue among the group of authors, Ferenczi, Freud, and, finally, the reader. This clarification is important for at least two reasons: first, as throughout the dialogues, the reader feels fully enmeshed as part of the dialogue, generating a sense of being immersed in the exchange of ideas. Second, it is clear that the authors are not trying to provide an introduction to the diary but rather a dialogue among many of the thinkers involved in this project with Ferenczi's thoughts and subjective processes. What the reader witnesses is the profound impact, through time and space, of Ferenczi's influence on contemporary analysts and analytic thinking. This is what is most notable about Authenticity and Reciprocity, which brings to the forefront Ferenczi as a crucial figure in the history of psychoanalysis.

The book is structured into three main chapters and an appendix (including a very helpful glossary in which the authors describe the way in which Ferenczi and Freud used psychoanalytic concepts in sometimes subtle but different ways). The first chapter includes three subsections beginning with an introduction to the general issues of technique and theory proposed in the diary. From the beginnings of his psychoanalytic investigations, Freud was deeply interested in applications of his findings to human history, our evolutionary history—how we became human, and historical processes seen in groups. Some of Freud's ideas on these matters led to him conflating and at times equating biological evolutionary development (phylogeny) and the development of the individual (ontogeny), and conflating the history of our biological evolution and group historical processes. The reasons for the lack of differentiation of these different phenomena, and why and how it will be necessary to properly work out their relationships to make further progress in this field of study, will be the theme of this essay. We begin with two recent books that revisit and revise two of Freud's major works on these topics. I would recommend both of them to anyone interested in the application of psychoanalytic findings to our biological evolution and group historical processes. Grace Andrews  

Reading requires you to surrender not only to the narrative, but to how that narrative is told. Surrender to the form of the story. This is the thrill and the pain of being a reader. To put it another way, a reader is the backseat passenger to an author's construction. That journey, to which one implicitly consents by embarking on readership, is alternatively pleasurable and tortuous, and very often a mix of both.

The reader's journey, once surrendered to, is deep and stunningly memorable in Richard Brockman's autobiographical novel Life After Death: Surviving Suicide. We call his book a novel, which implies fiction, because as Brockman points out, what one remembers is not veridical. Memory is selective, reflects what was reinforced (“Neurons that fire together, wire together”) (p. 22), and is reworked with each act of retrieval. Thus, every memoir aimed at transporting its reader to another space and time is part fiction and part historical truth. The good thing about autobiographical novels is that our inner skeptic, the one who screams this couldn't really happen, is quieted because the work is based on an actual life story. Brockman authentically plants that story in the Brooklyn of his childhood amidst the eccentric characters of his Jewish family, the allure of the pier on Sheepshead Bay, the bustling excitement of Lundy's restaurant, the punishing ride of the Cyclone at Coney Island, and the familiar street names: Ocean Parkway, DeKalb Avenue, and the Belt Parkway.

Judith L. Lewis, Professor, UVM Larner College of Medicine, Vice Chair of Education and Director of Resident Psychotherapy Training, Department of Psychiatry, University of Vermont Medical Center (UVMMC). UHC Campus, 1 South Prospect Street, Burlington, VT 05401.

Timothy Rice  

Karen Gilmore and Pamela Meersand of the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research have already jointly authored a comprehensive volume (Gilmore & Meersand, 2014) and an accessible handbook (Gilmore & Meersand, 2015) on human development, as well as a primer on play therapy (Meersand & Gilmore, 2017). In Emerging Adulthood, they offer yet another highly useful volume, now focused upon a unique psychodynamic exploration of this developmental phase.

Gilmore and Meersand orient the reader at the outset to the fact that emerging adulthood was proposed only a quarter of a century ago. Coined by developmental psychologist J. J. Arnett (2000), it initially spanned the age range of 18 to 24 years and was subsequently extended to the end of the third decade of life. The phase details the distinct tasks and challenges of young people that materialized in tandem with the technological novelty of the 21st century (Arnett, 2014).

The reader with curiosity or even doubts about the creation of a new developmental phase will appreciate the thoughtfulness of Gilmore and Meersand's persuasive supporting argument. The authors organize the writings of their psychoanalytic predecessors on adolescence (Blos, 1962, 1979; Erickson, 1968; Freud, 1958; Laufer, 1986; Laufer & Laufer, 1989), as well as those of contemporary writers on emerging and subsequent works in the psychoanalytic literature (Gilmore, 2019; Knight & Miller, 2017; Miller, 2017; Schechter et al., 2018; Shulman, 2017), to show how psychoanalytic developmental theory both anticipated the phase and provides an avenue for its thoughtful advancement.

Empty Heart Disease: Teaching and Learning in China

Jill Scharff and David Scharff

David Scharff

Email: jillscharff@theipi.org

Email: davidscharff@theipi.org

David Scharff   Cofounder, Former Codirector, Supervising Child and Adult Psychoanalyst, International Institute for Psychoanalytic Training; Past Chair, Overseas Programming, International Psychotherapy Institute, 6612 Kennedy Drive, Chevy Chase, MD 20815-6504, Bethesda, Maryland.
Empty Heart, applied psychoanalysis, psychoanalytic education, teaching and learning, trauma, transgenerational transmission, culture

Drawing on 15 years of experience teaching psychoanalytic theory and therapy primarily from an object relations perspective to Chinese psychotherapists onsite and online, the authors present their learning about Chinese culture, social history, and philosophy, and the Chinese way of communicating about emotional experience. Their essay is imbued with the Chinese use of metaphor and psychosomatic symbolization, particularly involving the heart. They elaborate on the Chinese concept of Empty Heart Disease, comparing and contrasting it to Western concepts from literature, sociology and psychoanalysis, namely spleen, anomie, dead mother, and schizoid, empty, false, and narcissistic self-states. They expand upon and extend the empty heart concept to various age groups and symptom presentations in China, illustrated by a vignette from individual psychoanalysis with a woman and three vignettes from applied psychoanalysis of a couple with no intimacy, a child with an obsessive psychosomatic symptom, and an adolescent school dropout who was self-harming and suicidal in response to academic pressure. Having emphasized the connection between symptom presentations and social life and times, they discuss the impact of trauma, its transgenerational transmission in China, and the impact of unprecedented economic growth and social change on individuals, couples and families.

[[RunningHead]]

Earthquakes in the Analytic Field: A Post-Bionian View of Negative Therapeutic Reaction

Caron Harrang LICSW FIPA BCPsa

Caron Harrang

ad2510 6th Avenue, Unit 2208 Seattle, WA 98121
Email: caron@caronharrang.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9459-1467

Caron Harrang  

alpha function, analytic field, negative therapeutic reaction, obstructive object, post-Bionian field theory, co-dreaming

This paper examines Negative Therapeutic Reaction (NTR) as proposed by Freud and extended by Kleinian and North American analysts before reinterpreting this well-known phenomenon through the lens of post-Bionian field theory. Employing Bion's concept of the obstructive object, the author suggests that NTR is characterized by a recognizable phenomenological sequence including impairment of the analyst's alpha function. When, as advocated by post-Bionian field theory, these reactions are understood as co-generated by the analytic group-of-two a more collaborative atmosphere develops in the analytic field. Just as pressure within the Earth produces movement of tectonic plates resulting in earthquakes of varying intensity, the author finds it metaphorical to consider NTR an emotional “earthquake” that can erupt following increasing intimacy in the analytic relationship. Clinical material illustrates how the analysand's experience changes depending on how the analyst listens to transformations occurring in the analytic field. In one case, the analyst employs a “you/me” mode of listening which hinders receptivity to communication via projective identification making NTR the dispatch of last resort. In a second, the analyst adopts a “we/us” mode of listening enabling her to employ NTR to restore a co-dreaming function as terra firma of the analytic relationship.

The Sounds of Silence: Embodied Registers, Erotic Reverie

Dianne Elise

Dianne Elise

5435 College Avenue, Suite 106, Oakland, CA 94618
Email: elised@earthlink.net
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1659-6908

Dianne Elise  

erotic, creativity, vitality, embodiment, Winnicott, reverie, sexuality

This paper engages a Winnicottian formulation of the analytic field to highlight the often unspoken, implicit erotic dimensions of analytic work. Material from an analysis with a very inhibited, emotionally constricted man shows both patient and analyst encountering difficulty in “locating” each other within the analytic field. Paradoxically, a felt sense of connection was also palpable, and possibly/impossibly erotic. Movement in the treatment required the development of what the analyst came to think of as an “erotic sonar”—a “sounding” in the erotic body of each participant that could indicate the place of creative connection. A sensing in the body for the “feel” of the analytic dyad became a kinetic reading of emotional closeness or distance, as well as indicating the subtle, and shifting, tones of the relationship. Emphasis is placed on bodily experience in a subjective registering of the erotic, both as sexuality and as a more general experience of passion, vitality, and creativity.

Edges of the Voice in Psychoanalysis

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

2960A Piedmont Avenue, Berkeley, CA 94705
Email: mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8681-136X

Mitchell Wilson  

I'm thinking about the edge of what you can hear.

—Paul Simon1

Psychoanalysis has always been conditioned, foundationally, on distance, because human living itself necessarily involves traversing spaces—I mean literal physical spaces—to get things done: whether it's to take shelter, obtain sustenance, find love, or simply talk. Some distance is traversed, always. And given that analysis is a human endeavor, we too must grapple with the fact that my body in space is located in a place other than where your body is located; the analyst is in a different place than the analysand.

We mediate this distance between bodies, between, that is, each of you in relation to each other and me in relation to you, in any number of ways. We might shoot a glance to someone seated in a row nearby. Or send a quick text to bridge this gap, to communicate something. I might shout across the BART tracks at the Rockridge Station in Oakland if I see a friend. If they hear me and I succeed in catching their eye, we wave to each other. “How's it going?” “Good. How about you?” “Fine.”

Distance allows for the creation of an edge, a limit, and therefore a space that can serve to demarcate inside and outside, here and there, sameness and difference, the one and the many. The entire field of lived experience—ground, sky, objects, living beings—exists in a holistic surround that we sense proprioceptively as we look, speak, and move about within this space. We tend to throw ourselves in front of ourselves automatically, procedurally, as we progress within this holistic surround. Fredric Busch  

Anne Erreich (2024), in her article “The Innate Capacity for Representing Subjective Experience: The Infant's Mind Is Neither Primitive nor Prerepresentational,” makes a crucial point about the lack of clarity of what is meant by unformulated or unrepresented states. More needs to be done to define these terms, including what is and what is not being represented, and how clinicians are making this determination. In addition, she notes that for something to be experienced in the mind it must be represented in some form. As she points out somatic states, often referred to in discussions of unrepresented states, are represented in the mind as the experience of bodily sensations.

What is most problematic in the paper is the misapplication of data from outside of psychoanalysis to push a particular psychoanalytic theory. Such a development occurred with the discovery of mirror neurons, which were seen as proof of immediate, nonverbal resonance (Gallese, Morris, & Migone, 2007), a popular concept at the time. Without going into great detail, our understanding of mirror neurons has become far more complex, and their basis for supporting any specific metapsychology or clinical approach has been questioned (Heyes & Catmur, 2022). In this case, Erreich has taken data demonstrating the “competent infant” to argue against unrepresented states and to support the defense model as the only legitimate basis for understanding difficulties bringing fantasies or feelings into conscious

“That's What You Say”: Response to Fredric Busch

Anne Erreich

Anne Erreich

170 West End Avenue, New York, NY 10023
Email: anne@erreich.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8073-3754

Anne Erreich  

I am grateful to Fredric Busch for taking the time to respond to my paper on mental representation. I will try to clarify my arguments and the ways in which they counter his response.

Peirce's three registers for representation, iconic, indexical, and symbolic are each “meaningful” in their own right; they are not hierarchically organized such that the first two registers necessarily defend against the third.

Busch makes an incorrect assumption regarding Peirce's three registers for representation. He assumes that subjective states in the iconic or indexical registers (e.g., somatic states and enactments) are not fully meaningful in their own right, that they require verbalization (“symbolization” in Peirce's terms) to acquire meaning. He mistakenly attributes to me this same belief, that the role of iconic and indexical representation is to operate defensively against the symbolic or verbal register. Thus, Busch writes,

some somatic states can be identified as emotions and fantasies. According to Erreich, if such bodily states are not represented in a meaningful way [in words], it is because of a defensive function that represses the conscious [verbal] experience of the associated fantasies and feelings [italics added]. (p. XXX)

Peirce's three representational registers can be transliterated from one to another, (e.g., the concept of “tiger” can be represented by the picture of a tiger, the footprint of a tiger, and the word tiger), but I do not propose that these instances represent some objective hierarchy with the verbal “symbolic” register at the top.

Anne Erreich, Psychoanalytic Association of New York, 170 West End Avenue, New York, NY 10023

Introduction to Anthony Grooms's “Hold On”

Kerry Malawista CFS

Kerry Malawista

New Directions in Writing. 9421 Thrush Lane. Potomac, MD 20854
Email: kmalawista@gmail.com

Kerry Malawista  

In his powerful essay “Hold On,” Anthony Grooms takes readers on a journey through historical and contemporary hardships, including the oppressive Jim Crow era and the emotional aftermath of the 2024 election, exacerbated by the shared burdens of the COVID-19 pandemic. Intertwining his own experiences of racism with the broader struggles against systemic injustices, Grooms underscores the importance of revisiting historical traumas while reflecting on how these narratives shape both individual and group identities. He highlights the remarkable resilience displayed in the face of racial oppression and political disillusionment, illustrating the human capacity to overcome obstacles and to heal through solidarity, hope, and community.

By revisiting the historical struggles for civil rights, Grooms encourages readers to learn from the past and to reconnect with the values of perseverance, unity, and joy in the ongoing fight against oppression and despair. His essay serves as both a personal testament and a call to action, reminding us of the power and responsibility each of us holds to rise from dark times and strive toward creating a more equitable future.

Kerry L. Malawista. CFS, New Directions in Writing. 9421 Thrush Lane. Potomac, MD 20854

Hold On!

Anthony Grooms

Anthony Grooms

Kennesaw State University
Email: tgrooms@kennesaw.edu

Anthony Grooms   Two subjects predominant Anthony Grooms's published work: social change, especially around the American Civil Rights Movement, and travel. These concerns are evident in the novel Bombingham and in poems that explore relationships between African Americans and Africans. Grooms is also the author of Ice Poems, a poem collection; Trouble No More, a story collection; and The Vain Conversation, a novel. He holds fellowships from Fulbright and Yaddo and awards from the Hurston-Wright Legacy Foundation, the Lillian Smith Prize, and the Black Caucus of the American Library Association. He is professor emeritus at Kennesaw State University.

“We have come through dark tunnels before,” my friend said. He had been crying all morning, and now walking the hilly path under pecan and oak trees, we were trying to decompress. The weather had turned drizzly and cool, after having been bright and unseasonably warm for a month. The change matched our moods. It was Wednesday, the morning after the 2024 election. The news had thrown us into an unexpected dejection.

My friend is a former journalist, so he had followed the returns beat by beat, until in despair he switched off the television. I, on the other hand, had avoided the news, the tension being too much for me. I was awakened by a groan from my wife at four in the morning. Inquiring as to what was wrong, she read from her phone. I rose and went to the toilet. My heart raced as I imagined the 4 years ahead. I went out onto the porch for deep breaths of fresh air. A void formed in my abdomen, just under my rib cage. It seemed so bottomless that it could not be filled with food, or drink or breath. Yet, it was roiling with emotion—disappointment, anger, and anxiety. I sat for the rest of the morning, dozing sporadically, and indulging in fantasies that somehow the results were all wrong. Somewhere on some forlorn stretch of road in Michigan was a truck load of uncounted ballots from a deep blue county.

Now, walking with my friend, keeping up a brisk pace, but occasionally slowing to greet neighbors who likewise seemed shell-shocked, my friend reminded me that “We have come through dark tunnels before.”

Rachel Boué-Widawsky  

Book Essay: IRED

In her rich and complex essay, “The Inter-Regional Encyclopedic Dictionary Series: Languages of One Psychoanalysis and Many: “Migration and Mutation of Concepts” Across the Globe—Part 1,” Eva D. Papiasvili offers an in-depth history and description of the Inter-Regional Encyclopedic Dictionary of Psychoanalysis (IRED). Papiasvili emphasizes the need for a pluralistic, multifaceted, multicultural approach to psychoanalytic inquiry. Strict adherence to singular conceptual frameworks, she argues, can obscure the richness of the discipline of psychoanalysis. According to Papiasvili, IRED, first conceived by Stephano Bolognini, has emerged as a collective effort bringing together analysts worldwide to contribute to an ongoing, multilayered dialogue. This international, interregional collaboration captures the global migration of psychoanalytic thought as analytic concepts traverse cultural boundaries and evolve through interaction with different psychoanalytic geographies. Papiasvili urges the reader to explore the many published IRED entries in order to delve into this lively psychoanalytic discourse and experience firsthand the dynamic evolution of psychoanalytic thought across language and cultural boundaries.

Anne Adelman, Editor, JAPA Review of Books. Rachel Boué-Widawsky, Faculty and Director, Respecialization Program, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, New York, New York

Reality, life, experience, concreteness, immediacy, use what word you will, exceeds our logic, overflows, and surrounds it.

—William James, A Pluralistic Universe

The complexity and richness of psychoanalysis worldwide are lost on the wider culture that surrounds us when we psychoanalysts attach ourselves too exclusively to one conceptual network, one school of thought, or one psychoanalytic language. The present state of the pluralistic polycentered multi-regional psychoanalytic landscape brings up the increased need for worldwide scientific communication among psychoanalysts of diverse psychoanalytic geographies, in scholarly dialogue with other disciplines, and in a clinical context with the otherness of patients of a progressively widened scope. New methodological tools are needed to recognize, represent, navigate, and ultimately transmit this plurality and complexity to future generations of psychoanalysts. In other words, we psychoanalysts first need to be able to talk to each other and translate between diverse psychoanalytic languages before we (re)claim our relevance to the wider culture. It is a strange feeling when traveling in a new place to see familiar buildings in slightly different settings. The golden arches of a McDonald's gleam against jungle palms or the desert's red aridity. Things that are well known and taken for granted appear new. Their familiarity draws attention to the newness of the landscape; the changed landscape, in turn, makes the traveler reconsider the familiar. I have already decided to use this text for our institute's Advanced Psychodynamic Psychotherapy program. I have also suggested it to my colleagues who oversee the curriculum for our institute's Analytic Training Program. A new book by Christof Koch is an event, because he's a major figure in the biological and philosophical renaissance of studies on consciousness that began in the 1990s and is in full swing today. If you watch his 2013 TEDx talk “The Scientific Pursuit of Consciousness” (TEDx Talks 2013), you'll find a humane, charismatic physicist and neuroscientist in his late 50s explaining his lifelong fascination with the subject. Koch cowrote two dozen scientific papers with the Nobel laureate Francis Crick (1916–2004), of DNA double helix fame, and has since published a huge number of such papers, and five books. Taking its title from a line in Wagner's (1882) Tristan and Isolde, Then I Am Myself the World is his sixth, an eclectic work written very much in the first person. Koch's main theoretical commitment remains Integrated Information Theory (IIT), a partly mathematical model according to which consciousness arises in, or is permitted by, any physical substrate whose structure has a sufficient amount of a very specific type of complexity. It was developed mainly by the psychiatric neuroscientist Giulio Tononi, with important contributions from others, especially Koch himself.

IIT is a powerful theory whose conceptual toolkit can, at least in principle, be used to assess any system's capacity for consciousness. Persuaded that it represents an important scientific advance in our understanding of how the brain's structure contributes to its powers, I remain skeptical that IIT amounts to a complete philosophical solution of the famous “Hard Problem of Consciousness” (Chalmers, 1997), which asks how mind can arise from matter. My first impression of The Pole, a 2023 novel by the Nobel Prize–winning South African author J. M. Coetzee, was that the volume itself had a peculiar appearance. It is notably thin for a novel and divided into six “books” of varying length, each prefaced by a page displaying a handwritten musical composition, possibly Chopin.

But the oddest feature is the enumeration of subsections, a sequence beginning anew for each book. These numbered components individually encompass a separate idea, change, or event. Some of the segments are of substantial length, while others are only one line, a single sentence or a phrase. To this reader, a slender volume with a prominent numbering scheme implicitly suggests aging. This could be a book about the numbered final days of life.

Then there is the title. One soon finds that “the Pole” is indeed a man with a name, but a name with many consonants that render it nearly unpronounceable (Witold Walczykiewicz). Moreover, referring to him as “the Pole,” which is done mostly in the early pages of the novel, has a distancing tone; he is unmistakably an “other.” Whether or not they are intended by the author, theories are naturally created by readers to explain the eccentric format of the book and the initial reluctance to engage with the Pole as fully human.

Like many psychoanalysts, Irene Cairo did not turn to fiction until late in life. After graduating from the University of Buenos Aires Faculty of Medicine in 1964, she moved to the United States and studied at the New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, where she is a member of the faculty. She is also a training and supervising analyst at the Contemporary Freudian Society. She has served as the North American Chair of the Ethics Committee of the International Psychoanalytic Association. Cairo is the author of more than two dozen journal articles, chapters, and book reviews, written in English and Spanish, on a wide range of subjects, including shame, envy, and trauma. Her willingness to be self-disclosing, and to admit her clinical mistakes sets her apart from other analysts.

Cairo's open-mindedness appears throughout her writings. The heroic, she argues in her book chapter “Voiceless Heroines: Deafened by Theory?” is defined by “courageous acts and nobility of character” (Cairo, 2019, p. 96). The chapter, part of Changing Notions of the Feminine: Confronting Psychoanalysts' Prejudices, edited by Margarita Cereijido, explores how Cairo freed herself from classical psychoanalytic theory, particularly ego psychology, that has limited her understanding of female patients. Growing up in Buenos Aires, Argentina, Cairo recounts how she has “no social or political early memories that do not involve a tyrannical government and a fear of the police” (p. 96). She was cautioned by her mother, “If you get lost, do not go to the police” (p. 96). Nor was she allowed to repeat ideas heard at home; even listening to clandestine radio broadcasts was dangerous. This volume, edited by Betti, Galiani, and Ruggiero, is a collection of papers resulting from the valuable work of several psychoanalysts on “Psychoanalysis and Public Role” of Società Psicoanalitica Italiana. This group comprises psychoanalysts who actively work in mental health centers within the Italian national health care system. The volume emerges from the proceedings of their meetings, gathers their testimonies and reflections, and offers living accounts of the challenges, emotions, pain, and commitments psychoanalysts encounter within public institutions. The volume does not aim to construct historiographical frameworks but instead seeks to provide firsthand accounts of the shared painful experiences of providing psychoanalytic care in these settings. Although this situation is somewhat foreign to American contemporary psychoanalysis, the review of this book aims to bring some insight into the precarious but necessary role of psychoanalysis in a larger socio-medical environment.

Steven Ablon

Steven Ablon  

For me an essential part of writing has been playing with ideas and words. Writing is the playground of the imagination. Over the years I have written articles playing with efforts to understand the process and therapeutic action of play in the analysis of children and adults (Ablon, 2000, 2001, 2014). These explorations started very early in my life.

Like many children, I spent enjoyable hours playing with my toys. I sent pirates on adventures and soldiers into epic battles. Figures reenacted tense meetings between the New York Yankees and the Brooklyn Dodgers. In my play there were surprising turns of events. I found that playing with words as in all play took me to surprising places. As Einstein said, “combinatory or associative play seems to be the essential feature in productive thought” (Hadamard, 1945, p. 144). Much later I learned that the element of surprise for the analyst and the patient provides conviction, which is not there when the analyst is too far ahead of the patient in shaping or interpreting material.

As far back as I can remember this powerful interest in play also extended to playing with words and eventually writing. As I wrote I discovered surprising things about the explorations of Vasco da Gama, and the present state of Nigeria. This also applied to creative writing. I recall a sonnet I tried to write according to my sense of Shakespeare. One couplet turned out to be, “I planned in secret how to kill another, only to find that I had killed my brother.” At the time there was a spark of discovery and recognition. In high school after a painful break up with a girlfriend I wrote a poem for English class about a girl being like a cold. It was a success in class, but I discovered my anger and the long miserable course of heartbreak.

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to Austin Ratner's “Attention Must Be Paid”

Kerry Malawista CFS

Kerry Malawista

New Directions in Writing, 9421 Thrush Lane, Potomac, MD 20854, USA
Email: malawistak@gmail.com

Kerry Malawista  

In his essay “Attention Must Be Paid,” Austin Ratner explores the fundamental human longing for connection—the desire to be seen, understood, and acknowledged. Drawing from a diverse tapestry of cultural and literary narratives, from Midnight Cowboy to Death of a Salesman, as well as historical contexts like World War II, Ratner weaves a deeply personal narrative rooted in his own experience of loss—the death of his father when he was 4 years old.

Ratner poignantly illustrates how trauma can become frozen in time, residing in a silent, aching space—devoid of time (a past or future) or words. Writing, for him, becomes an act of bridging that silent expanse, an effort to translate unspoken grief—the ineffable feelings surrounding his father's death—into a meaningful narrative. Through storytelling, he creates an imaginary world where trauma and emotion can be “named and tamed.” This process offers him a sense of mastery over his internal landscape, of both his characters' actions and the imaginary worlds' response. In this way Ratner's writing functions as a kind of alchemy—transforming chaos, fear, and pain into reimagined memories that can be both endured and shared, rendering them more manageable and, ultimately, more beautiful.

Kerry Malawista, CFS, New Directions in Writing, 9421 Thrush Lane, Potomac, MD 20854, USA

I never saw the man with another person, and I never saw him without his dog. The man was of advanced years, and so was the dog. It was a short-haired, short-legged, broad-shouldered dog, maybe some kind of a bulldog mix, who looked up from the sidewalk with yellowish, sickly eyes sort of like the suffering eyes of Saint Sebastian in one of those Renaissance paintings where he's tied to a pillar and stuck with arrows and looking heavenward. The dog moved very slowly and had an ugly growth on its back the size of a mushroom.

Then, one summer day, I saw the man alone, without the dog. And the next time too—alone.

We'd never conversed, I'd never heard him speak even once, but on one of those days after his dog disappeared, he suddenly erupted at me from out of the airtight silence that always enclosed him.

We were walking in opposite directions on the broad, sunny north sidewalk of Montague Street near the Promenade in Brooklyn and I was on the left side of the sidewalk, headed toward the water. I was rushing on some errand and as I crossed his path, he exploded at me, “Walk on the right!!!!” The command began at a conversational level and surged to max volume with its own uncontrollable momentum, like a cannonball. People stared.

This sort of thing happens in New York City. If psychological stress is a propellant for human conflict, it's ignitable at any moment in the constant friction of the big city. According to the last census, New York packs close to 30,000 people per square mile. No other American city touches even 20,000. From the Editors

Anne Adelman

Anne Adelman

JAPA Review of Books, 2 Wisconsin Circle, Suite 915, Chevy Chase, MD 20815
Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3372-3750

Anne Adelman  

If there is a theme that runs through this issue of the Review of Books, it is about the richness, intricacies, and surprises contained within a text. To read is to be willing to immerse ourselves within the pages, and experience the pleasure of a new idea revealed or a familiar one revised. Sometimes we can barely resist the urge to toss the pages aside in scorn or derision, decrying what we disagree with or despairing over the great divide between the words in the text and the ideas we hold most dear. In the pieces you find here, our reviewers have taken up a broad selection of books in ways that are unexpected, nuanced, multilayered, and thought-provoking.

This collection of reviews—from essays about the original psychoanalytic thinkers, the historical figures of psychoanalysis, to reflections of essayists, poets, memoirists and graphic novelists, to essays about the trauma of personal, communal and global grief and despair, to the history of mankind and the history of the individual, to the mind, its biology and neurology, to adult development to the development of psychosis, to reflections on the process and the meaning of writing itself—brings into sharp relief the extraordinary depth and breadth of psychoanalytic thought. We hope you will discover pieces that speak to you, move you, surprise you, and engage you in dialogue with our authors.

Book Essays

The first essay in our Book Essays section is a unique offering. We are delighted to bring to our pages a distinctive translation of Dr. Helí Morales's paper “Uncovered Violence: Feminicide and Cruelty Beneath the Open Sky: Beloved Mexico, So Beautiful, yet So Wounded,” with a special introduction by Mina Levinsky-Wohl and Gabriela Legorreta.

Gabriela Legorreta  

We had the privilege of interviewing Dr. Helí Rafael Morales Ascencio, the recipient of the 2020 Sigourney Award. The Sigourney Award, acknowledged as the most esteemed international honor in the psychoanalytic realm, carries a particular significance as it marks one of the few times this recognition was won by a Latin American psychoanalyst, more specifically, a psychoanalyst from Mexico.

In our conversation with Dr. Helí Morales, we delved into his psychoanalytic journey and the multiple significant projects that led to his well-deserved recognition. His pioneering efforts are focused on addressing the lack of institutional psychoanalytic services for low-income communities in Mexico. Linked to this work, he earned acknowledgment for his innovative approach in conceptualizing the emotional suffering of survivors of violence and their families in Mexico through a psychoanalytic lens and proposing ways to provide support.

Born in Guadalajara, Jalisco, Dr. Helí Morales embarked on a trajectory that led him to become profoundly involved in the field of psychoanalysis. Armed with a background in social psychology, he pursued two master's degrees—the first in psychoanalytic theory at Centro de Investigaciones y Estudios Psicoanalíticos in Mexico City and the second in psychoanalysis and the Freudian field at University Paris VIII. Dr. Morales then earned his PhD in philosophy and social sciences from L'École des Hautes Études en Sciences Sociales à Paris, with a

Mexico is a territory inhabited by many cultures. It is a mosaic of regional languages and diverse stories. But it is also a space of violence. The markets are drenched in blood. Mexico is one of the countries with the highest rate of femicides in the world. Among the many localities bathed in women's blood, Ciudad Juárez stands out as a territory commanded by drug traffickers and heavily identified with capitalist business interests. It is not only the border between Mexico and the giant of the North but also the line between poverty and opulence, power and dispossession, and life and death.

Since 1993, there have been 1,773 murders of women who were also sexually tortured. When I was 4, my mother taught me to read and write. Every afternoon, we would sit on the couch, side by side, with a black and white marbled composition book propped between us. Cocooned in maternal love, I learned phonetic families: cat, bat, mat; it, hit, bit. Using the words I could spell, together my mother and I made up little stories. Within a few months, I was reading independently. Magic—with my nose in a book, the heat of a summer afternoon or the tedium of a rainy one disappeared.

Guntrip, schizoid, regressed ego, womb fantasy, depression

In 1945 the Reverend Harry Guntrip, leader of the prestigious Salem Church of Leeds, was a man caught in the throes of spiritual and professional crises. Like the tragic figure of Father McKenzie in the Beatles' “Eleanor Rigby,” he'd been waging a desperate personal battle with emotional isolation while finding he was unable to save the lonely souls and “broken personalities” (Guntrip 1956, p. 11) he was increasingly encountering in his pastoral practice. His recurring bouts of depressive malaise, connected to a mysterious “pull out of life” (Guntrip n.d., p. 317) exerted from the depths of his psyche, had proven impervious to treatment from his Freudian psychoanalyst Hugh Chrichton Miller, while his pastoral psychotherapy practice was filling with “ill people who did not respond, as less ill people did, to the interpretation of their problems in Freudian terms of the Oedipus Complex” (Hazell 1994, p. 3). He found that these more deeply troubled patients “seemed to present problems of a much more obscure and profound origin” (p. 3).

[[RunningHead]]

Sure It Works in Practice, but Does It Work in Theory? Appreciating Fred Pine

Andrew Druck

Andrew Druck

Email: andrew.druck@nyu.edu

Andrew Druck   Faculty and Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program; Fellow (Training and Supervising Analyst), Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, 545 West End Avenue, Apt. 1A, New York, NY 10024.
Fred Pine, structural deficit, conflict, psychic structure, development of psychic capacities, interplay of theory and technique, contemporary Freudian psychoanalysis

Fred Pine is a major contributor to contemporary Freudian analytic work. He expanded the breadth of clinical psychoanalysis by showing how the analyst could integrate ever expanding perspectives in analysis, and he expanded its depth through greater insight into how development affects psychic structure and, thereby, the context within which unconscious conflict and compromise is experienced and processed. Both of these—his expansion of potential variables implicated in the process of dynamic conflict and his developmental focus on structural deficit—have led to a way of Freudian thinking that is highly assimilative and integrative. Pine's focus on integrating disparate points of view—not different theories, but clinical observations that are featured in different overall theories—illuminates clinical possibility and nuance. Pine's work leads to questions about the relation of psychoanalytic theory to analytic practice and the definition of contemporary Freudian psychoanalysis itself.

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to Susan Scarf Merrell's “Holding the Novel”

Kerry Malawista

Kerry Malawista

Email: kmalawista@gmail.com

Kerry Malawista   Contemporary Freudian Society, New Directions in Writing

In her essay “Holding the Novel,” Susan Scarf Merrell intertwines personal memory and insight, exploring the creative, safe, and supportive containment she aimed to provide in the writing program she devised. Through the recollection of her father's life and their shared moments, including his final breaths, she examines the art of holding and the legacy her father left of honoring kindness above all else. Like psychoanalysts, Merrell came to appreciate the profound connection between experiences of holding and the processes of growth and transformation—in both the artistic and personal spheres. Grappling with leaving the directorship of the program, she reflects on her fear of letting go, leaving room for herself and others to thrive.

Kerry L. Malawista, Contemporary Freudian Society, New Directions in Writing

Holding the Novel

Susan Merrell

Susan Merrell

Email: susanscarfmerrell@gmail.com

Susan Merrell   Author of Shirley: A Novel, which in 2020 became a major motion picture starring Elisabeth Moss and Michael Stuhlbarg. She is also the author of A Member of the Family and The Accidental Bond: How Sibling Connections Influence Adult Relationships. She teaches in the MFA in Creative Writing & Literature at The Lichtenstein Center of Stony Brook Southampton. Along with Meg Wolitzer, she directs the BookEnds Fellowship, a novel revision program.

Analysts understand the notion of holding in a way that creative writing faculty often don't. With this in mind, I set out to write an essay about the program I run, a post-MFA novel-polishing fellowship called BookEnds. I wanted to focus on the unique nature of the program, the way it endeavors to hold each writer's particular form of anxious fretting so that the writing can proceed with confidence. But each time I sat down to write the piece, I found myself remembering my father, who died in November of 2015, at the age of 85.

I kept thinking about a trip we took when I was 12, from New Haven to Manhattan. We were headed, I think, to see The Magic Flute. We got off the train and began walking to the Yale Club, a short distance away.

My father was not a brave man. Like many other immigrants of his generation of American Jews, he grew up both lauded for his intelligence and an impoverished outsider. He was naive about life, but he was gifted in mathematics and became a mathematical economist of international renown. His world was binary in the most fundamental sense: 0s and 1s. He was a romantic. He loved ballet and the opera, had season tickets to the Metropolitan Opera for many years. With him, I read all of Trollope, learned to cook, took many rambling walks around our neighborhood, through woods, and up mountains. Reader, cook, outdoor meditator: all those things I am that came from him. He was eager to please and almost always kind—with graduate students, with strangers, with his wife, and with his daughters. His heart was expansive. There seemed to be a seat inside it where you could linger quietly until you were complete again. He never failed to protect me from my own self-doubt, just long enough for me to realize I could master what was daunting.

Full disclosure: I write this review with a strong bias in favor of studying and highlighting the importance of the overlap between philosophy and psychoanalysis, particularly in the context of institutional psychoanalysis (I come back to this “bias” further later in the review). It was with enthusiasm that I embarked on reading and reviewing this handbook, edited by Richard Gipps and Michael Lacewing. Indeed, it fully merits its Handbook title, and the qualifiers, such as masterful and monumental, that come to mind when describing this work. With 42 chapters, 35 contributors, and eight subsections, the book covers the intersection between philosophy and psychoanalysis from two perspectives.

Gipps and Lacewing explain these perspectives in their overall introduction and section introductions to help situate this interdisciplinary field for readers from different intellectual backgrounds. Of the two perspectives, the first is historical: Section 1 examines the philosophical precursors of foundational psychoanalytic concepts, such as libido, intersubjectivity, and sublimation in the works of Hegel, Schopenhauer, and Nietzsche. Section 2 explores direct 20th-century engagements of psychoanalysis by philosophers such as Wittgenstein, Merleau-Ponty, Ricoeur, and Marx via the Frankfurt School. The second perspective, found in the latter six sections, is topical and covers domains such as aesthetics, clinical theory, religion, phenomenology and science, ethics, and politics and society.

The New World of Self makes the extravagant claim following the argument of philosopher John Riker that Heinz Kohut is the most important psychoanalyst since Freud. The authors argue that many clinicians, among them Jung, Erik Erikson, Winnicott, and Fairbairn, saw the need for new more humane, connected, and related forms of psychotherapy and psychoanalysis, but Kohut was alone in providing a coherent theory to undergird the transformations that were taking place in clinical practice.

The creation of The New World of Self compellingly reflects its message. It was written by Charles Strozier, Kohut's celebrated biographer, and three of Strozier's students and colleagues (Konstantine Pinteris, Kathleen Kelley, and Deborah Cher), two of whom were also Strozier's patients. The writing of a book by an analyst in collaboration with his patients would never have been possible in the post-Freudian world of ego psychology. In that world, patients didn't interact with their therapists and were assumed to know next to nothing about them. This was because analysis was believed to cure by interpretations that made the patient's unconscious conscious, and for this to happen, the analyst needed to be a blank screen upon which the patient projected unconscious infantile sexual and aggressive wishes. Mark Leffert's book raises important questions for psychoanalysis, of which I will focus on two in this review. How should psychoanalysis be aware of contemporary forms of technology's intertwining with psychological life? And how does psychoanalysis relate to questions of existential meaning?

Perhaps the most interesting aspect of Leffert's book is his discussion of technology vis-à-vis psychotherapy and vice versa. Many people across the world today spend inordinate amounts of their lives in cyberspace and, we could also say, in cybertime. Inhabiting cyberreality changes our lives and identities. Leffert claims that this “inhabited Space” (p. 99) has its own ontology: “To the … being of nature (res extensa) and the being of mind (res cogitans), we must now add a third being, res digitalis” (p. 160). This digital being involves “a different kind of Dasein” (p. 100), a term of Heidegger's that we can define as being-human-in-a-context. Dwelling in cyberreality is not escaping from reality; it is participating in a different register of reality. Leffert writes, “By its very existence, anything, including whatever is in Cyberspace, has some kind of reality” (p. 106). To give an ontology is to give an account of the general mode of being or reality of some kind of entity. We can do tool-ontology or art-ontology, for example, to ask what it is to be a tool or a work of art. Two decades after the psychoanalytic works about lesbians and gays by O'Connor and Ryan (1993), De Lauretis (1994), and Domenici and Lesser (1995) were published, I attended a social event for my institute community. A senior analyst approached the group of three lesbian candidates who had gathered for mutual support and comfort. The analyst regaled us with his idea of organizing a conference about love and homosexuality, planning to show that gays and lesbians love the same way heterosexuals do. He seemed confident that we would be excited by such a conference. My institute had admitted three out lesbians in the same cohort, progress toward acknowledging that homosexuality was not in itself pathological and reason to deny people psychoanalytic training. Yet the message from this senior analyst was clear. We could come into the psychoanalytic playground—as long as nothing about the game really changed.

As I contemplated Psychoanalysis and Homosexuality: A Contemporary Introduction, a volume in the Routledge series Introduction to Contemporary Psychoanalysis, I wondered for whom this introduction was intended. Perhaps the title provides a clue—homosexuality rather than queer—queer indicating more contemporary language. Associations abound with just one word. In the world in which I came out, my friends and I were lesbians, distinct from pre-Stonewall gay women. These designations carried implications for the closet, feminism, and gender roles. Homosexual, gay, lesbian, and queer are words front and back laden with the aftermath of second-wave feminism, gay liberation, the AIDS epidemic, and the emergence of a transgender movement. L'immensità

Robert Abrams

Robert Abrams

Email: rabrams@med.cornell.edu

Robert Abrams   Professor Emeritus of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medicine/New York Presbyterian, Department of Psychiatry and Division of Geriatrics and Palliative Medicine, 525 East 68th Street, Box 140, New York, NY 10065

The Italian film L'immensità is an enlightened and affecting portrayal of the suffering associated with “gender dysphoria,” a relatively new entity in the psychiatric lexicon first officially used in 2013 (American Psychiatric Association 2013). The film's focus is on the struggles of its principal character, a 12-year-old female at birth who was christened Adriana and is known en famille as Adri. However, Adri views himself as Andrea, a male name in Italy corresponding to the English Andrew, even if no one in his family calls him that. From earliest childhood, Andrea (Luana Giuliani) has endured a foundational conflict between his assigned gender and the male persona with which he wholeheartedly identifies.1

The setting for L'immensità is Italy in the 1970s, a place and time before contemporary concepts of gender diversity and trans experience were understood and accepted. With refreshing clarity, in L'immensità, Andrea's dilemma has to do with the most elemental features of personal identity. It is this straightforward but still sensitive approach that makes L'immensità a meaningful contribution to the current public conversation about gender. Much of the film's credibility derives from the real-life experience of its director and co-screenwriter, Emanuele Crialese, as a trans man. But in any case, gender-affirming medical interventions such as puberty-blocking drugs and surgical transitions would not have been available to Andrea in Italy in the 1970s. In L'immensità, Andrea considers himself to be properly male, dressing, thinking, and behaving like a preadolescent boy in the only way that feels natural to him. But there is a 1Consistent with the convention of describing gender-nonconforming persons as they see themselves and wish to be known, in this review the film's central character is referenced by his male name and corresponding self-identified pronouns.

What I'm Reading Now: Shakespeare's Twelfth Night and Old-Old Age

Warren Poland

Warren Poland

Email: warrenpoland@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0132-4897

Warren Poland   Psychoanalyst, Bethesda, MD, 9707 Old Georgetown Road, Apt. 2107, Bethesda, MD 20817

Shakespeare had a unique, even superhuman, gift for creating characters who spoke in a voice ever true to that specific person, whatever the age, gender, or status in society. He did that while simultaneously expressing the person's voice in poetry of such special beauty that now, more than 400 years later, we are still moved by his art and still use his phrases, his words, and his neologisms as part of our everyday modern speech.

Beyond that, Shakespeare's sensitivity to developmentally age-related conflicts and feelings is so keen and so accurate that when reading or watching Shakespeare's plays, his words continuously open fresh insight apt to the observer's pleasures and fears of the moment. Using Twelfth Night as my play in point, I have described the play's power to provide me with fresh understandings that had been beyond my reach until I heard his words:

Ever since I emerged from childhood during high school years, Twelfth Night (1971) has been for me a standard alongside which I could measure my growth, like the doorpost where my parents had earlier measured my changing height. I first found the play, a classroom assignment, pleasing, never suspecting I would go on through the years to see it performed many times, to read it repeatedly, and always find in it new levels that would mark and measure for me the alterations of my own unfolding life. It seemed as if the play extended its reach along with me as my life progressed. (Poland 2018, p. 153)

The play addressed my age-related inner emotional issues, and Shakespeare's writing brought me new emotional freedom by extending my limited insight.

This paper was made possible by John Pentz, my high school English teacher, to whom it is appreciatively and lovingly dedicated.

Warren S. Poland, Psychoanalyst, Bethesda, MD, 9707 Old Georgetown Road, Apt. 2107, Bethesda, MD 20817

Stephen Seligman

Stephen Seligman

Stephen Seligman   21 Vicksburg Street, San Francisco, CA 94114

Thinking about why I write opens many doors. As a writer, I think about matters like form, privacy, influence, communication, and the pleasure of words. As a psychoanalyst, though, I would be remiss if I omitted some of the more personal meanings: conflicts, anxieties, sublimations; family, cultural and institutional histories; and the times in which my career and personal identity have developed. Over the decades, though, with the advance of time, support from family, friends, colleagues and institutions, and a good-enough analysis, much of this has morphed into more autonomous pleasures, capacities, and talents.

I was a very successful student, eager to use my mind and be recognized for it. But this was not without conflict. My mother was my biggest fan throughout my childhood, which has been a great source of confidence and love. But as it was so “unconditional,” I found it difficult to sort out what about me was really worth admiring. My father often showed his care by being tough, perhaps filling this gap with a quotient of self-criticism and rebellion. All in all, I was unsure about recognition, competition, and ambition. (There are, of course, many ways to put all this.) Analytic culture offers fewer ways of measuring status and rank than many professions, and those that are offered—referrals, clinical reputation, and institutional and teaching positions—tend to be private,

[[RunningHead]]

Teleanalysis Does Not Have to be “Muted”: On the Crucial Role of the Analyst's Internal Frame in any Setting

Lena Ehrlich

Lena Ehrlich

Email: lenaehrlich@drlenaehrlich.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4820-2758

Lena Ehrlich   Training and Supervising Analyst, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; Visiting Faculty, Denver Institute for Psychoanalysis and the Florida Psychoanalytic Center. 555 East William Street, Suite 22C, Ann Arbor, MI 48104-4543
countertransference, transference, transitional space, resistance, in-office analysis, setting/frame, absence/loss, teleanalysis, psychoanalytic education, analytic identity

Given that practicing teleanalytically is relatively new and more widespread than ever, questions about how to practice and teach it effectively have increased and are more pressing than ever. To contribute answers to these questions, this paper addresses long-standing and persisting negative views of teleanalysis as an inherently muted, remote, and pale experience with reduced therapeutic effectiveness. I propose that this view of teleanalysis as inherently inferior to in-office analysis limits or even precludes its therapeutic usefulness because it allows analysts to avoid making analytic use of the disturbing feelings hiding within the experience of practicing teleanalysis. Through case examples, I suggest that when analysts do not take our negative views of teleanalysis at face value but instead consider them as symbolic expressions of our and our patients' anxieties, we can improve our understanding of our analytic functioning within the tele-setting and enhance our capacity to think and engage analytically effectively in any setting. I maintain that these considerations have important implications, not only for how experienced analysts practice, but also for how we treat and teach future analysts.

The “Fact of the Matter”: A Model for Working with Activated Internal Object Relations in Psychodynamic Couple Therapy

Barry Stern

Barry Stern

Email: bs2137@cumc.columbia.edu

Barry Stern   Clinical Associate Professor of Medical Psychology (in Psychiatry), Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons; Associate Director and Chair of Psychology, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, 122 East 42nd Street, Suite 3200, New York, NY 10168
couples, psychodynamic psychotherapy, object relations, projective identification, family systems

Extensive clinical scholarship has described the application of object-relational principles, particularly the operation of projective identification, to psychodynamic psychotherapy with couples. The author explores the way in which a more complete depiction of projective processes, one that incorporates each partner's intrapersonal management of multiple internal object relations, interacting interpersonally in the couple therapy process, can explain the escalating cycles of conflict between couples that are elaborated in the family-systems literature, and be helpful in understanding the object-relational substrate of chronic conflict in couples more generally. A description of how to map each partner's internal object world through the identification of these cycles in the early couple therapy process is elaborated in a theoretical model and illustrated with case material.

Re: “Two Cheers for Austin Ratner”

Austin Ratner

Austin Ratner

Email: austinratner@gmail.com

Austin Ratner   96 Joralemon Street, New York, NY 10017-1601

To the Editor:

I thank Professor Rudnytsky for the generous attention he's devoted to my book The Psychoanalyst's Aversion to Proof—first at the winter 2022 American Psychoanalytic Association (APsA) conference, to which he invited me as a speaker, and then in a long review in JAPA 71/4 (pp. 709-726). His knowledgeable, eloquent essay raises many points that bear further discussion.

In his commodious scholarship, Professor Rudnytsky covers a lot of ground and as such he only engages fleetingly with my central thesis. For that reason, and because he filters the Freudian argument of my book through a stringently non-Freudian perspective, his essay renders my book somewhat unfamiliar to me. JAPA's editors have graciously made an exception to their rules to allow me a brief response.

I hope that in addition to considering the points Professor Rudnytsky makes, JAPA's readers will consider my central thesis. If the thesis is right, it has implications for psychoanalysis. Mark Solms suggested my book “could help determine the future direction of American psychiatry and mental science,” praise I proudly stuck on the back cover. But my idea obviously can't help drive change if people don't consider it or don't know about it.

My idea, which I call “proof aversion,” is a straightforward corollary of the theory of repression. Repression, of course, opposes and delimits self-knowledge to protect us from emotions and memories that cause pain. The idea of proof aversion adds that the public exposition of psychoanalytic theories and insights can also be uncomfortable in a special way that invites unconscious mischief. Freud was acutely aware that speaking up about anxiety-provoking facts of emotional life, which people prefer not to hear, elicited others' resistance in the form of irrational criticisms, denial, and doubts. Kerry Malawista

Kerry Malawista

Kerry Malawista   CFS, New Directions in Writing, 9421 Thrush Lane, Potomac, MD 20854, USA

In her moving essay, Karen E. Bender confronts the profound sense of loss that envelops her after her father's death. She shares her bewilderment at navigating a world without him and the challenge of mourning the loss of someone so essential to her sense of self. Bender shares their treasured moments of connection, humor, and understanding, highlighting her father's role as a psychoanalyst who encouraged emotional expression in their family. Yet, she reflects on the paradox of his death, which introduced a haunting silence—a stark void of absence.

Seeking signs of his continued presence, Bender sits in her father's old office furniture, contemplating the many lives he touched throughout his career. A visit to the Freud Museum once again connects her to her father, as she finds echoes of his spirit in Freud's belongings, a reminder of the enduring impact he made as a psychoanalyst. By remembering and resurrecting memories, she discovers her antidote to loss. Ultimately, Bender recognizes her father's enduring influence, recognizing how he continues to live on in their shared love of language, laughter, and inquiry.

Kerry L. Malawista, CFS, New Directions in Writing, 9421 Thrush Lane, Potomac, MD 20854, USA

Book Essay

Psychoanalysis in the Classroom

In his book review essay on Deborah Britzman's work, Berman reminds us that Freud referred to education as one of the three “impossible professions,” along with psychoanalysis and governance. Berman highlights Britzman's sensitivity to the unconscious interpersonal and conflictual dynamics in the classroom and shows how Britzman draws on the phenomena of transference and countertransference to make sense of the sometimes subtle, sometimes blatant, resistances to learning. Berman appreciates Britzman's authentically personal voice—intimate yet well contained—as it allows the reader a glimpse of “warm pedagogy,” rooted in an understanding of developmental, psychosocial, and interpersonal influences. Berman writes, “Britzman ends the book by affirming that the quest for humane learning depends upon giving voice to one's emotional life, a challenge because it is suspended between the living and the dead.”

Jeffrey Berman

Jeffrey Berman

Email: jberman@albany.edu

Jeffrey Berman   English Department, University at Albany, Albany, NY 12222

In his late essay “Analysis Terminable and Interminable,” Freud (1937) famously referred to psychoanalysis as the third of the “impossible” professions; the other two, he adds, “which have been known much longer” (p. 248), are education and governance. Freud asks why they are impossible professions and then answers his own question: “one can be sure beforehand of achieving unsatisfactory results” (p. 248). Experienced teachers do not need to be reminded of the impossibility of their profession. Still, there have been surprisingly few psychoanalytic scholars who have explored the ways in which education arouses intense resistance in students and teachers alike. For this reason, Deborah P. Britzman's work has been noteworthy.

Britzman is a member of two of these impossible professions. She is the York Research Chair in Pedagogy and Psycho-Social Transformation, Distinguished Research Professor of Education, Emeritus, at York University, Toronto, and a psychoanalyst. To add to her credentials, she is a Fellow of the Royal Society of Canada. For years, Britzman has been writing challenging books about how to transform impossible classrooms into sites of creative learning. She is one of the leading theorists in the area of education, and her commitment to Freud has never wavered.

In her memoir Memories of a Chaotic World: Growing Up as the Daughter of Annie Reich and Wilhelm Reich, Lore Reich Rubin, a psychoanalyst who trained at the New York Psychoanalytic Institute, essentially authored her own case study. Born in 1928 to Wilhelm and Annie Reich, Rubin takes us on her journey from her earliest childhood to her father's death in prison in 1957. The richness and poverty, advantages and strains of her life as a child of well-known psychoanalysts inspire many of her engaging stories.

Rubin started to write, “just as a creative expression of some vivid early memories” (p. xv). With encouragement from colleagues, and from Wolfram Ratz of the Wilhelm Reich Institute in Vienna, Rubin, at 93, decided to write and publish her story. Perhaps one of Rubin's goals was to resurrect her father's reputation and place in psychoanalytic history. Her memoir offers more than that, however; it gives a child's perspective on this history—a close-in view of growing up with a prominent, difficult father, a disinvested mother, and ultimately a ruptured family, during the lead-up to World War II. Learn to love the questions. Then try, as if you were one of the first men, to say what you see and experience and love and lose.

—Ranier Maria Rilke, Letters to a Young Poet

Philip Lister has written an important short good book, one which requires courage to read and will remain with the reader for a lifetime. His extraordinary writing evokes a parallel empathic experience in the reader, who will enter a young child's mind during a terrible time in her life when she and her family were forced to ask all the hard questions. The reader will bear witness to her family's utter respect for the truths in those questions as they slowly begin to mirror and struggle with the inner world of their very sick child. Although this may be one of the most painful books that most readers will ever read, Lister's narrative is committed to respecting the truths in a mortally ill child's questions as he acknowledges for himself and for his family that the loss of their child has changed their understanding of themselves, and of their relationships, lives, and purposes in the world. While this is a story of Liza's illness and death, it is also a story of a courageous family's survival and resolution, and can, therefore, leave a reader hopeful and inspired to face such things directly.

Milton Wexler (1908-2007) is a forgotten figure in the history of psychoanalysis. Alice Wexler's book rehabilitates him while contextualizing the controversies and ruptures that marked his life. Wexler is never mentioned locally at the analytic institute where he belonged for almost 15 years, from 1951 to 1965. His name and clinical activities were erased. Perhaps the amnesia illustrates a lack of interest in local psychoanalytic history, perhaps a willed ignorance. This book is a necessary corrective. Without having met Wexler, I write this essay with admiration for the man and his work. He tried to ascend mountains that needed to be climbed and that other colleagues avoided or sidestepped. He attempted to be effective in the treatment of extremely disturbed patients and, subsequently, with those who have Huntington disease. Challenging and transgressing traditional analytic “rules” in ways considered deviations, rogue, or inappropriate, he was a doer, not just one who interpreted. His practice and writings on the real relationship between the patient and the analyst, while anticipating the perspectives of contemporary relational analysis, were dismissed by many psychoanalysts as “nonanalytic” or “wild.” Milton Wexler was a pioneer and an experimenter.

Owen Lewis

Owen Lewis

Owen Lewis   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry in Medical Humanities and Ethics, Columbia Vagelos, College of Physicians and Surgeons, 11 East 87th Street, New York, NY 10128

A Cielo Abierto, a film by Mexican sister and brother codirectors Mariana and Santiago Arriaga, and written by their father, Guillermo Arriaga, follows the genre of the adolescent coming-of-age bildungsroman. The film premiered at the 80th Venice Film Festival. In this genre of literature and film, a trip, in this case a road trip, structures the plot. The film opens with a father and his younger son, 12-year-old Salvador, driving to the northern mountains of Mexico for a hunting weekend. There is a relaxed, easy, and loving relationship between father and son, as well as a sense of foreboding. While driving, the father asks for a drink. Salvador looks but can't find one. The father takes his eyes briefly off the road to reach back for a drink. There is a head-on collision with a tractor trailer. The father dies; his son lives.

Two years later, the family has been reconstituted. The mother has married a widower neighbor, and the family now includes an entitled stepsister, Paula. Her boyfriend is always there, clingy and looking for moments to make out. Both brothers have been displaced in their family. They are angry about this unwelcome realignment and the loss of their father. The older brother, Fernando, is obsessed with car wrecks. He prowls automobile scrapyards, developing an uncanny ability to assess each wreck for the number of fatalities in each crash. Eventually, Fernando discovers the vehicle in which his father died, exacerbating his anger. Salvador is sullen and preoccupied with catching precisely timed glimpses of Paula undressing. Salvador claims to not remember anything of the crash despite his brother's interrogations of him. Fernando has collected all the information he possibly can about the accident, including the name, address, and profile of the truck driver involved in their father's death.

It was Grade 7, and I had a paper to write for English class. I don't remember the topic, but I do remember that I had done the reading and some thinking on it—I just hadn't written anything. And the hour was getting late, on the night before the paper was due. I sat down to write, and rather quickly was well into it, almost done. But by then the hour was truly late, even for a night owl like myself. I was falling asleep at my desk as I wrote, so I quickly jotted a conclusion down and went to bed. For weeks we didn't get our papers back—unusual for this teacher. Finally, she handed out typed copies of a paper to all of us. (This was long enough ago that we all wrote out our papers longhand and the typed copies were reproduced on a mimeograph machine.) She said this was an example of the sort of paper she was looking for, and pointed out a number of its good qualities. Its one not so good quality, she noted, was that it kind of tailed off at the end, with no proper conclusion. I couldn't fully recognize the handout as my essay until she said this, and then I knew!

I've recounted this story a number of times to friends and family for its humorous and mildly self-aggrandizing aspects, but as I thought about it in preparation for writing about why I write it stuck me that the incident, taking place at the start of adolescence, set a template for writing that followed. In future efforts I front-loaded my inhibitions and self-doubts, waiting till the last possible moment, and then went at it, quite enjoying myself as the words largely came of their own accord. This type of procrastination with sudden release is not the only way inhibitions show up, but it's a not uncommon variety.
