On Not Having it All: Exploring the Fetishization of Trans Women by Heterosexual Men

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

Email: alemma@mac.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9465-9054

Alessandra Lemma   Queen Anne Street Practice, 55 Queen Anne Street, London W1G 9JR, United Kingdom
gynandromorphophilia, transgender pornography, seminal anxiety, female body, misogyny, online pornography

The appeal of transgender pornography, especially involving “preop”(erative) trans women, has steadily increased placing it in the top six most searched categories. In this paper I explore one unconscious function of the “pre-op” trans woman sexual fantasy that I have observed in some young heterosexual gynandromorphophilic men (i.e., men sexually drawn to the MtF “pre-op” body) who use transgender pornography and/or escorts. In contrast to a psychoanalytic reading of gynandromorphophilia in which the “pre-op” trans woman's body is understood to be “missing nothing” because she has breasts and a penis, I suggest that what is psychically compelling and sexually arousing for some men is quite the opposite: because the “pre-op” trans woman has a penis, she therefore has no vagina/womb. It is the triumph over what is missing and that should be there. The penis in lieu of the vagina/womb is evidence of her damaged procreative potential. In these cases, I propose that the sexual preference exposes the man's underlying womb envy. The “preop” trans woman is projectively identified with a felt-to-be “infertile” part of the man. The phantasized damaged woman's procreative body temporarily relieves the man of his sense of inadequacy and anxieties about his capacity to create and enliven. I illustrate this dynamic with clinical examples.

Letter to the Editor

Donald Moss MD

Donald Moss

Email: dbmossmd@gmail.com

Donald Moss   New York Psychoanalytic Institute, 80 University Place, 5th Floor, New York, NY 10003

I was very grateful to learn that my book Psychoanalysis in a Plague Year would be reviewed in JAPA. Still, I feel compelled to write with a clarification. I wrote the book during the first year of COVID-19. The book consisted of one sentence recalled after each clinical hour of the working day. At the end of the day, I collated the sentences, lining them up vertically so that they resembled verse. The particularity of this structure mattered to me. As I say in my preface, I wanted to give a sense of having overheard these sentences, more than having listened to them. They were not gathered by way of a clinical ear, but instead by way of a companion's ear. We were in it together. I linked the day's sentences as one might link the scattered sentences one can overhear on a railroad platform. Not one of the day's cluster was put together as a “poem.” While the reader is, of course, free to read the book in any way he or she chooses—even against the author's intention—I also want to insist on the integrity of its structure, on a founding idea that generated an unexpected form, unexpected content, and, I hoped, an unexpected experience for the reader. In turning the book into “poems,” we dissolve the unexpected element. And here, I would say, were we to read the book as if it were a book of poems, we would conclude, in fact, that they really weren't very good. Oh, well….

Donald Moss, MD, New York Psychoanalytic Institute, 80 University Place, 5th Floor, New York, NY 10003

Email: dbmossmd@gmail.com

Beth Kephart's beautifully woven, lyrical narrative, “Inheritance,” tells a story of memory, loss, and what we carry with us after a loved one is gone. Through a series of poignant anecdotes, Kephart grapples with the remnants of her grandmother's life and the fleeting nature of memory. Particularly how personal objects and stories can evoke deep emotions and connections that seem to reach across time. Writing about these memories helps her reclaim lost stories and keep the spirit of those who have passed alive. As she finds comfort in her memories, Kephart highlights the bittersweet experience of discovery, as new insights into the family's history come to light, blending past and present.

Inheritance

Beth Kephart

Beth Kephart

Beth Kephart   National Book Award finalist, is a paper artist and the author of some 40 books, including the forthcoming Tomorrow Will Bring Sunday's News: A Philadelphia Story (Tursulowe Press, April 2025), which reclaims the mythology of her grandmother. Find her on her Substack, The Hush and the Howl, and at

URL: bethkephartbooks.com

.

She was already dying. She had been, for so long, dying. A word that won't explain her. Her narrow row home in southwest Philadelphia explained her. Her cool concrete back stoop, one alley shy of the hoop swish and sneaker squeak of the community basketball game. Her basement: cave dark damp, clotted webs, arranged according to the roll-top desk and the movie magazines and the orange wedding dress hanging from a wire. A photograph of a girl on a horse. Hats, I think, or I imagine, my memory strange and scrambling.

The day she died the sky received her. I looked up when my father said the words and something streaked or blurred, and that is where, in the years afterward, I wanted to believe I'd find her.

Look up.

Not long after my grandmother died, the things she'd chosen, worn, or made were disappeared by the woman my grandfather met at a foxtrot hall and married, it seemed to me then, within hours. (My brother says not hours. My brother says years. I don't remember years.) My grandmother's history was hauled to the curb and abandoned. Her Easter Sunday platters. Her Frank Sinatra radio. The defiant hues of her wedding dress. The paintings she'd found at flea-market fairs and nailed, at escalating heights, to the staircase wall. My grandmother had been radically foreclosed by the woman who'd replaced her, and in my mother's rush to save any material remnant of her mother's life, she salvaged only a silver hair brush, a few hooked rugs, cracked photographs, a Bible, and a tiny pearl on a slender chain.

My mother. Lore.

That pearl was placed in my trust. For years after, there was the sky above and a white globe at my neck, until, one day—I was a mother by then—it was gone. The slim chain broke and the white globe fell, and I searched for it, I searched for it, winter through spring then summer, the sky above roiling in and rumbling out. No pearl among the dry-rot leaves 331

Book Essay

Settler Colonialism: Where to Now?

We are pleased to offer Leon Hoffman's timely book essay, “Settler Colonialism: Where to Now?” In the essay, Hoffman notes that individuals address conflict by seeking balance among conflicting desires, wishes, and needs. He asks, “Can that principle be applied to nations? Can compromises be found, or does might always make right?” One could argue that current times add a sense of urgency: What can psychoanalytic thought bring to bear on this question? According to Hoffman, Freud and Einstein explored the struggle between instinctual aggression and societal control, questioning whether human evolution could curb destructive tendencies. Hoffman writes that while authors Cavanagh and Veracini examine global historical examples of settler colonialism, he perceives an ideological bias in their work that portrays European settlers negatively while disregarding similar historical movements by non-Western civilizations. Kirsch's book challenges this approach, arguing that such a selective focus might foster division rather than provide a nuanced understanding of historical conflicts and their resolutions. Hoffman reflects,

Scholars in the field of postcolonial and settler colonial studies need to recognize the complexities of contemporary realities. As they explore ways to redress the historical wrongs that have been inflicted on Indigenous peoples, can they appreciate that there is an interdependence among all people in the contemporary world?

Anne Adelman, Editor, JAPA Review of Books, 2 Wisconsin Circle, Suite 915, Chevy Chase, MD 20815

Conflict is inevitable within individuals, among individuals, within compatible groups, between groups, and between nations. As individuals, we all utilize compromise formations, trying to find the most adaptive solution as we try to balance conflicted desires, wishes, and ideas within ourselves and between us and others. Eugene Mahon complements his three prior books, Rensal the Redbit (Mahon 1990), A Psychoanalytic Odyssey (Mahon 2014), and Bone Shop of the Heart (Mahon 2017), with a masterful contribution in Such Stuff as Dreams: A Psychoanalytic Inquiry. The first of his books is a fairy tale for children and adults alike that was reviewed as a poetic summary of his explorations on the psychoanalytic process (Balashova-Shamis 2017). The second book is a collection of essays representative of this scholarly work (Schwaber 2017). The third book directly portrayed Mahon's poetic capability (Schwaber 2019). Mahon's latest book is a second collection of formal papers focused on his unique contribution to the psychoanalytic understanding of dreams.

One factor that makes this volume unique among the many psychoanalytic studies of dreams is Mahon's extensive experience writing about singular occurrences, which, he proposes, demonstrate the universality of dream work. Vividly portrayed jokes, dreams within dreams, parapraxes, poems, puns, the uncanny, tricks, and cartoons become instances of the dream work's special flourishes and flair. To Mahon, these phenomena represent extraordinary efforts to maintain the dreamer's sleep. Exploring the latent content surrounding these manifestations produces a bountiful yield for exploring the unconscious conflicts of the analysand. Mahon complements these pieces through expositions on broader aspects of dreaming, including its developmental aspects, its evolution through the stages of life, and its integration with affect and fantasy.

Timothy Rice, 1090 Amsterdam Avenue, 13th Floor, Suite A, Office 5, New York, NY 10025

In recent years, contemporary psychoanalysis has shifted from focusing primarily on the intrapsychic to emphasizing the intersubjective domain. While this transition remains significant, contemporary psychoanalysis is now marked by a pivotal evolution from binary to nonbinary, and epistemological to ontological (Ogden, 2019) modes of thinking. It is rare to find an author like Levine who naturally embodies this paradoxical way of thinking. Pluralistic thinking and an existentialist perspective form the cornerstone of Levine's approach in her book Risking Intimacy and Creative Transformation in Psychoanalysis.

A central theme in Levine's work is the influence of trauma and creativity on the challenge of forming a coherent personal narrative. By developing a space for openness, permeability, and honesty from both patient and therapist, Levine explores how a close psychoanalytic process can help patients recover their memories, enhance their creative agency, and take control of narrating their own life stories. Levine's case studies are notable for their lack of neatly resolved endings. Instead, she presents her doubts, using them as tools for authentic writing and deeper therapeutic engagement, which provides the elements needed for a new perspective on the past and a reimagining of the future.

Explicitly addressing complex topics is challenging for the therapist; however, silence can render the therapist a silent witness. Since the publication of Psychoanalysis and Behavior Therapy (Wachtel, 1977) early in his career, Paul Wachtel has been one of the leading voices in the effort to build bridges between the oft competing theories and practice of psychotherapy. His current book is a capstone to a long career devoted to integrating and finding commonalities among differing voices in the field, as he draws liberally from psychoanalytic, humanistic, cognitive-behavioral, and attachment-based traditions. Here, he pulls few punches. He is deeply critical of clinicians and theorists who remain embedded among their like-minded colleagues and have professional identities that are limited by the silos they inhabit. From the beginning, Wachtel took a different approach, seeking out opportunities to train with Wolpe and other leading behaviorists. Throughout, he maintained the keen eye of an outsider intent on learning from masters in the field, including those whose work was largely discounted by Wachtel's psychoanalytic colleagues.

Although well aware of this history and having studied with Wachtel in graduate school, I find it striking that these internecine struggles continue to inspire such intense feelings among the different theoretical camps. As a developmentalist and psychologist who began his career working primarily with children and adolescents, these debates can feel like old news. One of the pleasures of getting old(er) is the opportunity to witness the growth of thinkers whom we admire. For me, watching Dr. David Olds develop his ideas over time has been inspiring and educational. Dr. Olds is a professor of clinical psychiatry at the Columbia College of Physicians and Surgeons. He is also a training and supervising analyst at the Columbia Psychoanalytic Center. For much of his career, Dr. Olds has made an effort to integrate psychoanalysis with other disciplines, including neuroscience, semiotics, evolutionary psychology, genetics, primatology, and biology (to name a few). He has been an editor of Neuropsychoanalysis, a journal that also strives to make bridges between neuroscience and psychoanalysis. Since the early 1990s, Dr. Olds has organized a course for psychoanalytic candidates that encourages them to explore the interface between psychoanalysis and other disciplines. He has been in this game for decades.

Now, in this book, we have an accessible overview of Dr. Olds's lifetime “explorations” of the interactions and crossovers of the mind and brain in psychoanalysis and neuroscience. These previously published articles, now collected in one volume, present a window into the progress of “neuropsychoanalysis” over recent decades. Dr. Olds has allowed his psychoanalytic understanding of depth psychology to be tweaked by his searching dives into other disciplines. He is one of the few people in our field capable of bridging the divide, and this book allows us to retrace his journey.

Uneasy Listening: Notes on Hearing and Being Heard by Anouchka Grose and Robert Brewer Young challenges assumptions about listening and its value. Its authors raise a host of questions regarding listening's potentiality and materiality; however, I am not sure that Grose and Young stay with the trouble long enough for us to discover new truths about listening (and its attendant unease).

Young, a respected luthier, spends his life listening to the wetness of wood, to the sing of a saw as he constructs high-end instruments for some of the best violinists and cellists in the world. Grose lives quite a different listening life, spending her hours listening to patients in Lacanian analysis. Uneasy Listening is a brief book structured as an ongoing dialogue between two individuals who spend their lives listening. The reader listens to them trying to figure out how to talk—and listen—to one another about their listening. In my doctoral dissertation in philosophy, I explored the complexities and difficulties of the self/other relationship using the methodology of psychoanalytic listening (Hitchcock, 2023). Hence, this topic has fascinated me for over a decade, and I came to Uneasy Listening with strong hopes.

In the philosophical and psychoanalytic literature, thinking about listening holds several axiomatic truths, such as that listening is disruptive to the subject's—and potentially the culture's—status quo (Lipari, 2014; Nancy, 2002/2007; Szendy, 2008). I hoped to quote from his paper on the goals of analysis—the one that famously opens, “In doing psycho-analysis I aim at: Keeping alive. Keeping well. Keeping awake” (Winnicott, 1965, p. 166)—but I could not remember the exact title. Was it “Aim” or “Aims”? “Psychoanalytic” or “Psychoanalytical?” With a hyphen or without? I entered “Winnicott aims psychoanalytic treatment” into Google, assuming it would provide a quick answer. I was surprised to be presented first with the following “AI overview”:1

Donald Woods Winnicott (1896–1971) believed that the purpose of psychoanalytic treatment was to help patients:

Stay alive, well, and awake

Be themselves

Express themselves

Enjoy the analysis

Do analysis because they need it

Talk about misinformation. I ultimately clarified that the paper in question was “The Aims of Psycho-Analytical Treatment,” first presented in 1962, but the thought-terminating glibness of this list made me grateful for the reflective book I had just read. I'm in the back of an open military truck, although I am not a soldier. Soldiers in the truck are throwing heavy sandbags of some kind out the back of the truck as it moves through countryside. Each one explodes upon landing, and I feel troubled by the damage it causes. I calm myself by reminding myself that I am not one of the ones throwing sandbags.

As I awaken from this dream, it instantly dawns on me that I am the dreamer who created the scene. I am with my wife in a small village in Guatemala, near Lake Atitlán. I know immediately that the soldiers in my dream are American, and that my nation has sent them to Guatemala. Indirectly, I am the cause of the damage I see in my dream. My plans for the day change immediately. I must write about my dream and all that it brings up for me.

On that day, I wrote for 5 or 6 hours nonstop. On the previous day, my wife and I had visited the site of a recent deadly mudslide that buried a village, killing over 1,000 people. The highly traumatized guide who showed us around also took us to the Peace Park, a site commemorating the murder of 13 Maya civilians by Guatemalan soldiers during the former right-wing dictatorship in Guatemala. In my dream, I tried to disavow responsibility for the military violence in Guatemala and the shooting of Maya civilians. But I knew that U.S. military aid and secret Central Intelligence Agency involvement had supported the Guatemalan government.

The fact that my father was here on earth, and then not, was preposterous. So I kept looking for him. The hellish year that preceded his passing, in early 2024, did not prepare me, really, for the ongoing quiet of our discussions, the fact that they were now just one way. The fact that he died at age 93 made it seem even more impossible, not less—he rallied, saying, “We'll beat this!” with the authority he used his whole life, raising a defiant fist even as his body slowly shut down over a year. At a certain point, it seemed hard to believe it would even happen. Maybe it just wouldn't!

And then I sat in my parents' kitchen after his death and waited for him to walk in to sit at the table, flipping through the Los Angeles Times, eating his concoction of oatmeal, blueberries, soymilk, orange juice, and a slice of avocado. I tried to hear his raucous laugh, a pure expression of delight when I told him something funny—his laugh was also an endorsement of the world's absurdity. I imagined him saying, fiercely, “I hate him more!” when we discussed Trump during his first term, which somehow lifted the load of my anger. And I imagined him telling me, “Trust your keppy”—his support on most endeavors would pour down like a bucket of light.

He passed, and suddenly when I wanted to talk to him, there was air.

I liked to think of myself as a rational person, one who believed in science, evidence, verifiable facts, but when he died I went full Ouija board, trying to locate signs of him. There was the spooky fact that the nurse who came to declare his death was named Rose—the same name as his oldest sibling, who had died as an infant, over 100 years before. I saw a truck in my neighborhood that said Sara Lee Moving, the name (spelled the same way!) of another sibling. Of course, I thought—his siblings were helping him along to some other place. I wasn't sure how the logic of this worked, but my brain, in this new reality, was determined to construct any logic, no matter how loopy.

Model Minority and Its Discontents

Kris Yi

Kris Yi

131 N. El Molino Ave Suite 220 Pasadena Ca 91101, USA
Email: krisyiphd@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0009-0001-6157-2286

Kris Yi  

Asian American experience, Model Minority stereotype, Battle Hymn of the Tiger Mother, intergenerational transmission of historical traumas, American Dream

Model Minority expectations for Asian Americans have been in circulation for more than seven decades, and their grip on the Asian American psyche endures to the present day. In previous papers, the author addressed Asian American compliance with these expectations as a trauma-based, identifying-with-aggressor adaptation to the anti-Asian racism in American society: Reeling from the long history of racial discrimination that culminated in the incarceration of 120,000 individuals of Japanese origin during WWII, Asian Americans embraced compliance and model citizenship as a way of survival. In this paper, I highlight another source of the Model Minority adaptation, namely, the unresolved historical and cultural traumas (i.e., genocides, wars, poverty, authoritarian governments) carried by immigrants from Asia and intergenerationally transmitted to their Asian American children. Intergenerational transmission of large-scale historical traumas has not been explored in the psychoanalytic literature, especially for Asian immigrants. Some immigrants unconsciously attempt to resolve their historic traumas carried from Asia by a manic pursuit of the American Dream. For illustration, I use extended clinical examples and the writing of Amy Chua, a well-known Chinese American author who extolls a harsh parenting style to coax academic performance from her children.

As of 2025, there are 25.8 million Asian Americans in the United States, accounting for nearly 8% of the total population (USA Facts, 2025). While the Asian American population is the fastest-growing racial minority group in the United States (Pew Research Center, 2024), they remain largely unknown to Americans in the American consciousness. Recent surveys, asking 5,000 American adults to name one famous Asian American, yielded a quarter of respondents unable to do so (The Asian American Foundation, 2024). When people were able to name them, the two most popular names, for 3 consecutive years from 2021 to 2023, were Jackie Chan (who is not American) and Bruce Lee (who passed away 50 years ago). Kamala Harris was the third most popular, followed by Lucy Liu. After Kamala Harris, no South Asians were listed among the top 20 names (The Asian American Foundation, 2023).

The Korean American poet Cathy Park Hong (2020), in her book Minor Feelings, insightfully summarizes the Asian American experience of invisibility by comparing it with a collection of minor feelings that are too trivial to be taken seriously. It is thought that this predicament is in part due to the American racial dynamic dominated by Black-White racial tensions rooted in the slavery of Black people and its damaging, traumatic legacy in the present day, leaving little room for consideration of the experiences of non-Black people of color. In this paper, the author, a South Asian American female analyst, discusses her technical approach to working with patients across ethnic, racial, religious, and sociopolitical boundaries. She also describes the trajectory of her analytic career over three decades: both the challenges and the high points. Certain problems in organized psychoanalysis, especially about minorities, are briefly discussed.

Indian Boomers in The USA: Getting Old, Depletion Melancholia, and Adaptive Strategies

Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar

100 Grays Lane, Apt. 608 Haverford, PA 19041
Email: salman.akhtar@jefferson.edu

Salman Akhtar  

immigrant, depletion melancholia, reparation, sublimation

Fresh sources of suffering arise as the mostly successful and affluent Indian immigrants face late middle age, empty nest, retirement, death of peers, and getting old. Having actualized the dreams of professional and financial success does bring them contentment, but with a gnawing sense of renewed geocultural dislocation. Bodily changes, diminished sexuality, letting go of children, lack of familiarity with the prevalent normative patterns of parenting adult offspring and engaging with grandchildren, losing friends “back home” to illness and death, and getting old themselves in their adapted homeland leads them to experience “mental pain” and “disorienting anxiety.” These can turn into “depletion melancholia.” This paper describes the syndrome, the desperate defenses against it, and the possibility of thwarting it by the powers of creative sublimation and reparation offered to the good internal objects damaged by the process of immigration.

Gurmeet Kanwal

75 South Broadway, #4-4408, White Plains, NY 10601, USA
Email: gkanwalmd@gmail.com

Gurmeet Kanwal  

South Asian, Asian, identity, race, ethnicity, embodied, culture

This essay explores the experiential nature of identity through a social, psychoanalytic, phenomenological, and personal lens. Using reflections on racial, ethnic, and cultural categorization—particularly the tension between “Asian” and “South Asian” identities—the author examines how identity functions as both an internal construction and a reaction to external social mirrors. Drawing from ideas related to embodied cognition, relational psychoanalysis, and existential issues, the paper argues that identity is neither fixed nor merely socially imposed but is dynamically created through lived experience. The essay also considers the emotional stakes of identity loss, the role of racialized and ethnicized experience in fragmenting selfhood, and the fragile longing for continuity in a world of shifting categories.

Yin Li  

Asian American, racialization, racial character structures, identification, disidentity

In this article, the author shares observations and preoccupations that have arisen in the process of founding The Asian American Center for Psychoanalysis. They make use of these experiences as an entryway into the various terrains of Asian American: as a term, as a category, as subject, and as a field of study. They highlight the potentiality of affinity based spaces for backgrounded and dissociated self-states to reemerge, rethink Asian American as “disidentity”—as theorized by late Cuban queer scholar José Esteban Muñoz, introduce racial character structures, and offer four vignettes as explorations into the polymorphous becomings of Asian American.

This Asian Will Not Be Analyzed

Joseph Reynoso

Joseph Reynoso

10 W. 86th Street, Suite 1A, New York, NY 10024
Email: drjsreynoso@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0831-9185

Joseph Reynoso  

Filipino America, jouissance, enjoyment, identity, Philippines, Asian American, exclusion, desire, resistance

There is an idea misattributed to Freud that a certain “race” (e.g., the Irish) cannot be analyzed. One of the problems of this statement is its particularity. Thinking a certain race cannot be analyzed not only accepts the perniciousness of categorical racial difference, but also overlooks a more central idea Freud investigated in clinical work: that an unconscious antagonism inherent to subjectivity is the motivation and resistance to know oneself. This is the premise of this paper, which illustrates samples of Filipino American experiences in the psychoanalytic consulting room. Several vignettes are presented, in which fantasies of the author's identity are conjured to facilitate and hinder the analysis. The intrapsychic, interpersonal and sociohistorical conflicts featured in these cases reflect not only how the Philippines can be positioned in American and Asian imaginations, but also how investments in the identities of self and other reveal how we relate to our constitutive lack. It finally reflects on the jouissance (enjoyment) taken in identity's rewards, exclusions and impossibility.

Transfigurations: Ornamentalism and A Decolonial Theory of Magic

Sien Rivera MD

Sien Rivera

1301 Taylor Street, Suite 6J, Columbia, SC 29201
Email: siensrivera@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0009-0003-9249-8888

Sien Rivera  

psychoanalysis, ornamentalism, magic, transfiguration, colonialism

How might one begin to heal the transgenerational wounds of colonization in psychoanalysis or psychotherapy? Legacies of coloniality— structures of power, labor, and subjugation—continue to cause psychic harm, particularly at the intersections of gendered and racialized bodies. In the Euro-American imagination, the flesh of the feminine Asiatic body in particular becomes the site for all manner of constructions, including but not limited to inorganic objectification: commodities to be possessed, repulsed, or destroyed. This article seeks to apply Anne Anlin Cheng's concept of ornamentalism to create a path toward healing the pain of colonial objectification by complicating the categories of human and object themselves. It does so by proposing a decolonial theory of magic that allows for transfiguration to occur, illustrating these concepts with a clinical vignette of a transfeminine Asian American therapist and a transfeminine Asian American patient.

The Collapsing and Reclaiming of Subjectivity: Asians in the United States

Pratyusha Tummala-Narra

Pratyusha Tummala-Narra

140 Commonwealth Avenue, Chestnut Hill, MA 02467
Email: tummalan@bc.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6179-3823

Pratyusha Tummala-Narra  

dissociation, internalization, conflict, defense, psychotherapy, trauma

The lack of mentalization of Asian American experiences has contributed to a collapsing of subjectivity within psychoanalysis and broader society. Racism directed against Asian Americans is often minimized or dismissed. This paper explores psychoanalytic perspectives on the invisibility, dissociation, and repression of Asian American experiences, the problem of homogenization, and the discomfort with multiplicity within the United States. Drawing on clinical vignettes and research findings, the author describes how subjectivity is collapsed and reclaimed, with an emphasis on the experiences of Indian Americans.

Komal Gupta

Komal Gupta

207 Washington Street, #471031, Brookline, MA 02445
Email: kgupta@mindsightservices.com

Komal Gupta  

How often have we, as psychoanalytic clinicians, considered the ethnocentric limits of psychoanalysis when working with a patient of a different cultural upbringing? How familiar do we have to be with patients' cultural backgrounds to effectively treat their suffering? How much have we deeply contemplated how our own cultural subjectivity influences what we perceive as good or bad about our patients' ways of relating? How did psychoanalytic theories become disentangled from their historical and cultural surround and privileged as universally valid? Sudhir Kakar offers a compelling exploration of these essential questions, drawing on clinical, historical, and personal examples in the Indian Jungle from the perspective of his Indian, Hindu cultural imagination. Kakar proposes that we all have a cultural imagination, which is largely unconscious, that shapes our distinct worldviews, ideas about reality, and beliefs. He theorizes that our cultural imaginations develop through the “emotional stirrings of the heart and body” (p. xiv) from early childhood through the ongoing exposure to lives, songs, and stories of a large group of people who share a common culture. He emphasizes, though, how mental representations of culture have largely been neglected and unexplored in psychoanalytic discourse. By not analyzing our cultural imaginations and those of our patients, Kakar believes we lose the rich tapestry of cultural upbringing and how culture is ingrained in how we cognitively organize and understand experiences, what we consider real and unreal, how we know our own needs and desires, and how we are touched, held, and fed.

Aries Liao  

In The Wedding Banquet, director Andrew Ahn and cowriter James Schamus deliver a poignant and timely reimagining of Ang Lee's 1993 queer classic (Lee, Peng, & Schamus, 1993), anchoring it firmly in the lived experiences of Asian American queer individuals. This film foregrounds the Asian American coming-out experience, bringing to the screen a layered portrayal of queer Asian American characters navigating love, family, and cultural obligations.

Set in post–Marriage Equality America and against a rising tide of political backlash against LGBTQ+ rights, the film explores the complexities of love, identity, family, and belonging through a distinctly Asian American lens. From the very first scene, we're immersed in the specific cultural terrain of queer Asian American life, where tradition, immigration, and chosen family intersect in tender, complicated ways. At a moment when gender and sexual identities—and the legal protections that support them—are under renewed attack, The Wedding Banquet offers a life-affirming, emotionally rich portrayal of queer Asian Americans navigating intergenerational tension and love. Through masterful storytelling, Ahn captures the nuanced interpersonal struggles and the quiet yet familiar bidding of love that define many immigrant family dynamics.

Kenji Miyamoto  

Spanning over 20 years of collaborative effort beginning in the early 1990s, Eng and Han's Racial Melancholia, Racial Dissociation: On the Social and Psychic Lives of Asian Americans provides a compelling analysis of the unique psychological experience of Asian Americans. In many ways, witnessing Eng and Han's work culminate in this book has a deeply personal meaning for me, serving as a form of parallel process to forming my own identity as both an Asian American and a clinical psychologist who was first inspired by their article, “A Dialogue on Racial Melancholia” (Eng & Hand, 2000). This article was a primary source for my doctoral dissertation analyzing the impact of media stereotypes on Asian American masculinity. To have the opportunity to come full circle from an aspiring student to having the privilege of reviewing their book is both an honor and a psychic homecoming.

Skillfully blending psychoanalysis and critical race theory, Eng and Han present multiple case histories across two generations, Generation X to Generation Y, to demonstrate the psychic mechanisms of racial melancholia and racial dissociation for which the book is named. Through this process, they explain the changes in demographics within the Asian American population, as well as the ways we toggle back and forth between the history of the racial subject and the subject of racial history.

Tina Nguyen  

On Earth We're Briefly Gorgeous by Ocean Vuong is the debut novel from an award-winning Vietnamese American poet, written as a letter to his mother, which explores themes of race, class, addiction, power, violence, and intergenerational trauma with a palpable rawness. Vuong describes the book as a “phantom novel” as it is dedicated to the “phantom readership of the mother, of my family” (Spiegel, 2017/2022), who have low literacy and are unable to read the book. This phantom dedication highlights the painful division between parents and children within immigrant families, both divided concretely by language barriers but also by cultural perspectives. Even as he seeks to share the deepest parts of his life with his mother, giving life to what was once unspeakable, memorializing words to paper, yet knowing she will never read them. Perhaps it is this knowing that provides the necessary distance, shielding him from maternal rejection, that allows Vuong to bear the risk of vulnerability.

While this book illustrates a multitude of themes related to the profound loss and reorganization of refugee families, each exquisitely impactful in its own right, this review will focus specifically on one salient aspect: the experience of child immigrants and refugees, as depicted in the author's narrative. Child immigrants are thought to be more receptive and adaptable to their new country than adolescents (Ying et al., 2001). However, their reliance on caretakers who may be psychologically overwhelmed makes them more susceptible to upheaval (Akhtar, 1995).
